title: Quantum Otto machine with $q$-deformed Pöschl-Teller oscillator
authors: Collins O. Edet, Norshamsuri Ali, Rosdisham Endut, O. Abah
abstract: We study the impact of the potential parameters of the q-deformed modified Pöschl-Teller potential on the thermodynamic performance of a quantum Otto cycle, where the $q$-deformed modified Pöschl-Teller potential serves as the working substance. Analytical expressions for the energy spectrum and wave functions are derived, enabling a systematic investigation of heat exchange, work output, efficiency, and coefficient of performance. We show that $q$-deformation modifies the energy spectrum and creates distinct performance regions in the ($q$, $Δ$) parameter space. Low ($Δ$) and high ($q$) favour optimal heat engine efficiency, whereas high ($Δ$) and low ($q$) improve refrigerator performance. The heat engine efficiency peaks in the low-($Δ$), high-($q$) regime. These results highlight the q-deformed modified Pöschl-Teller potential as a versatile and tunable platform for exploring potential parameter-driven effects in quantum thermal machines.
summary: This paper explores how the deformation of a Pöschl-Teller potential can be used to tune the performance of a quantum Otto engine and refrigerator. It shows that by adjusting the deformation parameter and potential depth, one can switch between optimal heat engine and refrigerator regimes. The findings suggest that q-deformed potentials provide a versatile platform for designing tunable quantum thermal machines.
main_problem: Investigating how the parameters of a q-deformed modified Pöschl-Teller potential, specifically the deformation parameter q and the potential depth and width, affect the thermodynamic performance of a quantum Otto cycle.
main_result: The study identifies distinct performance regions where low-depth and high-deformation parameters favor heat engine efficiency, while high-depth and low-deformation parameters optimize refrigerator performance.
method: The authors use an analytical approach involving a factorization method to derive energy spectra and wave functions, followed by statistical mechanics to calculate thermodynamic variables within a quasi-static Otto cycle framework.
model_or_system: A quantum Otto machine using a q-deformed modified Pöschl-Teller oscillator as the working substance, governed by a Hamiltonian involving a deformation parameter q, a width parameter alpha, and a depth parameter Delta.
key_observables: Energy spectrum, wave functions, heat exchange (Qh, Qc), work output (Wq), efficiency (eta), and coefficient of performance (COP).
important_parameters: q (deformation parameter), alpha (potential width), Delta (potential depth), and reservoir temperatures (Th, Tc).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a non-relativistic framework and a quasi-static cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes the potential shape for varying parameters; Figure 2 shows energy eigenvalue variations; Figure 3 presents an entropy-temperature diagram; Figures 4 and 5 show thermodynamic performance (work, heat, efficiency, COP) for engine and refrigerator modes.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the derivation of the q-deformed potential and its energy spectrum, followed by the application of the quantum Otto cycle framework, a systematic parameter analysis of engine and refrigerator regimes, and a discussion of the results.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for researchers in quantum thermodynamics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how tuning the underlying algebraic deformation and potential geometry can control the operational phases and efficiency of quantum thermal machines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The q-deformed modified P¨oschl–Teller potential as a function of x for different: (a) α = 0.5 (dotted red line), α = 1 (dot blue line), and α = 2.0 (solid black line) (b) q = 0.5 (dotted red line), q = 0.7 (dot blue line), q = 0.9 (dot green line)and q = 1.0 (solid black line) (standard P¨osch-Teller potential). c ∆= 0.9 (dotted red line), ∆= 1.8 (dotted blue line), ∆= 2.7 (dotted green), and ∆= 3.6 (solid black line). Other parameters used: q = 1, ∆=1.5, and α=1.

Figure 2. Energy Eq n of the q-deformed P¨oschl–Teller potential as a function of (a) q (b) α and (c) ∆, for different energy level n. Here, n = 1 (dotted red line), n = 2 (dot blue line), n = 3(dot green line) and n = 4 (solid black line). Other parameters used: q = 1, ∆=2, and α=1.5 (a) and α=0.5(b).

Figure 3. The schematic of the Entropy-temperature (S-T) diagram of the quantum Otto cycle. The quantum thermal cycle comprises of two adiabatic strokes (B →C and D →A) where it is decoupled from the thermal reservoirs, and two isochoric strokes (A →B and C →D) where the engine is coupled to two thermal baths at temperatures Th and Tc, with Th > Tc.

Figure 4. Quantum Otto cycle as a heat engine. (a) Heat exchange with the hot reservoir, Qq h (b) Heat exchange with the cold reservoir, Qq c (c) Total work output, Wq (d) Efficiency ηq, as function of the depth of the potential ∆(3.7 < ∆< 5) and the deformation parameter q(0.8 < q < 0.9). Parameters used: αc =0.5, αh =1.118, Th =5, and Tc =1.

Figure 5. Quantum Otto refrigerator cycle (a) Heat exchange with the hot reservoir, Qq h (b) Heat exchange with the cold reservoir, Qq c (c) Total work output, Wq (d) Coefficient of performance COP q as function of the potential well depth ∆(0.9 < ∆< 1) and q(0.80 < q < 1). Parameters used: αc = 0.5, αh = 1.118, Th = 5, and Tc = 1.
